summary: DHH argues that productive company culture requires a clearly set pace for shipping work, using 37signals' Shape Up methodology as his model: six-week cycles, two-person teams, with unfinished projects getting canceled. He connects this to Elon Musk's Twitter takeover, framing it as shock therapy for a company with an extraordinarily low pace where employees could go years without shipping anything. While he disagrees with Musk's specific work ethic demands, he finds the argument for dramatic pace correction compelling.
key_points: A clearly set pace is the foundation of productive company culture — when pace is wrong, everything else falters
37signals proves major features can ship in six weeks with two-person teams, sustainably and without burnout
Twitter exemplified extreme lethargy: a former 37signals hire had spent two years at Twitter without shipping anything
Musk's Twitter takeover is shock therapy — an entrepreneur's urgency colliding with a culture rewarded for inaction
DHH rejects Musk's specific methods (in-office mandates, insane hours) while endorsing the underlying goal of raising the pace
money_quotes: A productive company culture is one with a clearly set pace.
Projects that fail to ship within that timeframe are canceled.
This person had spent over two years at Twitter and never shipping anything.
It was mockups, meetings, and then eventual cancelations that propelled life in the product group at that time.

An entrepreneur and engineer used to making big moves quickly, and broadly being rewarded for such impatience with progress and success, and a company and its staff used to making no moves slowly, and also broadly being rewarded with such sloth by continued existence and employment.

I don't subscribe to Musk's concept of a work ethic (in-office only, insane hours, sleeping on the floor), but I think there's a cogent argument that this kind of shock therapy is simply what's needed to drag Twitter out of the hole its been in.
Musk took over a Twitter set to one, and now he's turning it up to eleven.
key_insight: Every company needs a clearly set shipping pace, and Twitter's acquisition drama illustrates what happens when an organization's pace has been allowed to stagnate for years — shock therapy may be crude, but the underlying correction is necessary.
